Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Alif, Lam, Meem. (1) As we have been explaining at various places, this is an independent sentence in our view. That is: this is Surah Alif, Lam, Meem. This is the actual Qur'anic name of this Surah. The preceding Surah also has the same name. This serves as a contextual indication that, in terms of the central theme (amood), there is no major difference between the two Surahs. Based on the philosophy of this universe explained in the previous Surah—and the resulting glad tidings of divine support and ultimate victory in this world and the hereafter for the Messenger and his companions—the disbelievers had mocked these promises, using a specific historical event (detailed ahead) as a pretext. In their view, they had found a physical testimony to refute the messengership of Allah's Messenger (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), over which they rejoiced greatly, while this joy naturally caused grief to the Muslims. In this Surah, using that very event as an introduction, the Qur'an has explained all those aspects about which the disbelievers were in illusion due to their ignorance, and which also caused some confusion among a group of Muslims because they had not fully grasped them. Thus, this Surah serves to re-substantiate the contents of the previous Surah with fresh arguments and to dispel all doubts that arose or could arise in this regard. This shows that the subject under discussion in both Surahs is identical. The only difference lies in the perspective and the method of argument. In both of them, the messengership of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is substantiated through the truth of the principles he presented.

The Romans have been defeated. In the nearest land. But they, after their defeat, will overcome. Within three to nine years. To Allah belongs the command before and after. And that day the believers will rejoice. (2-4)

'The nearest land' (adnal-ard) refers to the land of Syria and Palestine, which was directly adjacent to the land of Arabia. At that time, this region was ruled by the Romans, who were suffering from severe internal discord. Taking advantage of this, the Persians attacked them and expelled them from these territories. This event occurred around 614 CE—i.e., in the sixth or seventh year of the Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) commission.

'But they, after their defeat, will overcome' (Wa hum min ba'di ghalabihim sayaghliboon). 'Ghalab' (defeat) is annexed to its object. This is the prophecy made by the Qur'an: although the Romans have been defeated at this moment, this defeat is temporary; they will soon overcome the Persians again.

'Within three to nine years' (fee bid'i sineen) means this political revolution, which the Qur'an is predicting, will not take long. It will occur within a few years. Although the prefix 'sa' in 'sayaghliboon' also denoted the near future, it carried a type of vagueness, which was removed by adding the condition 'within three to nine years' (fee bid'i sineen), making it clear that this revolution would occur within the next decade at most. The word 'bid'i' in Arabic is not applied to a number greater than nine. Specifying this duration was necessary because, as we noted, the polytheists had treated this event as proof of the correctness of their own philosophy, which naturally grieved the Muslims. Through this emphatic prophecy, the Qur'an reassured the Muslims: 'The joy your opponents are displaying by treating this event as proof in their favor is temporary; it will soon turn into grief.' On the other hand, it placed a touchstone before the polytheists to evaluate the truthfulness of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), serving as a grand proof of his prophethood. History records that about nine years after this event, Heraclius reorganized the Roman forces, inflicted a crushing defeat on the Persians, recovered his territories, and even captured many Persian regions. Thus, the Qur'an's prophecy was fulfilled word for word.

'To Allah belongs the command before and after' (lillahil-amru min qablu wa min ba'd) means whatever happened (the defeat of the Romans) occurred by His command, and whatever will happen (their ultimate victory) will also occur by His command. Such major revolutions in the lives of nations do not occur merely due to accidental shifts of fortune, but by the command of Allah, which is always based on truth and justice. When the Romans developed those corruptions that make a nation deserve His warning, they could not escape it merely because they claimed some true beliefs or performed religious rituals. Worldly rituals alone do not preserve the social life of a nation; rather, the real factor is the individual and collective character produced by those beliefs. When that character was lost, the hollow nature of the Romans led to their defeat at the hands of the Magians. Now, the victory promised to them in the near future will also occur by His command, because they will reform their internal affairs and make themselves worthy of His support against the Persians.

'And that day the believers will rejoice' (Wa yawma'idhing-yafrahu-l-mu'minoon). On that day, the Muslims will rejoice—both because the nation closer to them in religion (the Christian Romans) will be victorious, and because a grand prophecy of the Qur'an, which serves as a major proof of the Prophet's messengership, will be fulfilled.

It is noteworthy that although the Romans did not follow the true teachings of Jesus and had developed various corruptions in their beliefs, the Muslims still sympathized with them against the Magians, and Allah commended this sympathy. Islam has established its international relations with other non-Muslim nations on this very principle: the closer a nation is to Islam in its beliefs and philosophy, the more the Muslims' sympathy will be with them against others.

In the victory of Allah. He victories whom He wills, and He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful. (5)

'In the victory of Allah' (bi-nasrillah) can be connected to 'they will overcome' or 'the believers will rejoice.' In the first case, the meaning is that the victory they (the Romans) will attain will be through the support of Allah, for He supports whom He wills, and none other can support anyone. It should be noted that His support is based on His justice and wisdom; therefore, He supports only those who deserve His help in His law of justice. Referencing the attributes 'Al-Aziz' (Mighty) and 'Al-Rahim' (Merciful) here defines the nature of His will: He is dominant, so none can stay His hand, but He is also Merciful, so His will is always based on justice and mercy. He does not use His power to pitch nations against one another merely to watch their destruction on earth.

[It is] the promise of Allah. Allah does not fail in His promise, but most of the people do not know. (6)

'The promise of Allah' (Wa'dallah) is an absolute, emphatic promise. Allah will never violate His promise, but those whose vision is limited to the apparent causes fail to see the hidden hand that runs these phenomena; therefore, they do not understand this reality. The promise referred to here is not merely the promise of the Romans' victory, but also the promise of victory and dominance for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims—the very promise the Quraysh had tried to refute using the Persian victory. After commenting on that event, the Qur'an emphatically reaffirms the primary promise under discussion in this Surah. Further explanation of this will follow in verses 47 and 60.

They know what is apparent of the worldly life, and they, of the Hereafter, are unmindful. (7)

This explains the blind spot of those outward-looking people: first, they observe only the apparent aspect of this worldly life and fail to recognize the true Disposer within it and His attributes, treating it as a mere play. Second, they are completely unmindful of the afterlife, and without the afterlife, it is impossible to understand this universe as a purposeful, just creation. The repetition of the subject in 'And they, of the Hereafter, are unmindful' (Wa hum 'anil-akhirati hum ghafiloon) emphasizes that they are the ones who are truly unmindful of the afterlife. Although the Arab polytheists did not explicitly deny the afterlife, they had established such a system of polytheism and intercession that the afterlife held no practical value for them. The Qur'an clarifies that none should assume they believe in the afterlife in any capacity; the actual reality is that they are completely unmindful of it.

Have they not reflected within themselves? Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them except in truth and for a specified term. And indeed, many of the people, in the meeting with their Lord, are disbelievers. (8)

In this and the subsequent verses, the text draws attention to the signs of the universe to support the previous statement. If they were to reflect within themselves, they would realize that Allah did not create the heavens, the earth, and what is between them in vain, as a mere play, but created everything with a purposeful truth (bil-haqq) and for a specified term. Since every object in this universe carries a purpose and is bound to a specified term, how can it be assumed that human beings—the noblest creatures within it—were created in vain, without any purpose? This requires that a Day of Judgment be established, in which everyone is called to account, rewarded for his good deeds, and punished for his evil deeds. Otherwise, this grand universe would be a purposeless play, and humans would be left without any responsibility—a concept contrary to the wisdom displayed throughout the universe. This same theme is expressed elsewhere as: 'And they reflect on the creation of the heavens and the earth, [saying], "Our Lord, You did not create this in vain; exalted are You [above such a thing], so protect us from the punishment of the Fire."' (Al 'Imran: 191). 'Have they not reflected within themselves' (A-wa-lam yatafakkaroo fee anfusihim) shows that this wisdom is opened only to those who reflect within themselves with complete sincerity to find the truth, not to those who blindly follow others or demand physical signs merely to dispute.

'And indeed, many of the people, in the meeting with their Lord, are disbelievers' means despite this obvious reality, many fools assume they were created merely for worldly pleasures and will perish into nothingness, without any Day of accountability before their Lord.

Have they not traveled through the earth and observed how was the end of those before them? They were greater than them in power, and they tilled the earth and inhabited it more than they have inhabited it, and their messengers came to them with clear evidences. And Allah would not have wronged them, but they were wrongdoing themselves. Then the end of those who did evil was the worst, because they denied the signs of Allah and used to mock them. (9-10)

To substantiate this reality, the text points to the lessons of history: if they were to observe the ruins of their own land, they would see what was the end of nations who possessed far more military power and agricultural and architectural capabilities than them. This refers to 'Ad, Thamud, and Madyan, whose stories were detailed in previous Surahs. Their messengers came to them with clear signs, but they closed their eyes to them. Consequently, Allah seized them in punishment, and this was not an injustice, but absolute justice, because they ignored the warnings of the messengers and mocked His signs. The Quraysh are urged to reflect on this history: does it suggest that this world is a playground where everyone can do as they please, or does it prove that Allah has always dealt with nations in accordance with His law of justice? This historical evidence proves the latter, which also requires that a Day be established to call every individual to account and reward or punish them accordingly.

Allah originates creation; then He repeats it; then to Him you will be returned. (11)

In the previous verses, the purposeful nature of the universe was used to substantiate the law of recompense. Now, this and the subsequent verses state the actual reality which everyone must face. It states that Allah originates creation, and He will repeat it. These words combine both the claim and its proof. The claim is that He will repeat creation, and the proof is that He originated it. Since He originated creation without any difficulty, repeating it is simple for Him—even simpler than the origination. This is the response to the deniers who treated the Resurrection as highly improbable: when origination is not improbable, why should repetition be so?

'Then to Him you will be returned' (thumma ilayhi turja'oon) dispels another illusion of the deniers. They treated the Resurrection as highly improbable, and even if they assumed its occurrence, they thought they would return to their idols, who would intercede to save them. The text states: 'Dismiss this false hope. Everyone must return to Allah alone; on that Day, none other can become a refuge.'

And the Day the Hour is established the criminals will despair. And there will not be for them among their "partners" any intercessors, and they will [then] be in their partners disbelievers. (12-13)

'Iblas' means to be completely despondent and dumbstruck. It states that on the Day of Judgment, when the actual reality is exposed, those who relied on the intercession of their idols will be completely dumbstruck. They will see that none of the partners they worshipped can intercede for them.

'And they will [then] be in their partners disbelievers' (Wa kanoo bi-shuraka'ihim kafireen) means they will turn into deniers of the very partners they are ready to fight for today. As explained in Surah al-Qasas, the polytheists will adopt different attitudes at different stages of the Resurrection: sometimes calling upon their partners, and at other times denying them. In their panic, they will try every excuse, but the Resurrection is not a place for excuses; it is a place for facing realities.

It is noteworthy that future events are described using past-tense verbs ('will despair' / 'will be') to emphasize their certainty, as if they have already occurred. This style is frequently used in the Qur'an's descriptions of the Resurrection.

And the Day the Hour is established - that Day they will separate. And as for those who believed and did righteous deeds, they will be in a garden delighted. But as for those who disbelieved and denied Our verses and the meeting of the Hereafter - those will be in the punishment brought forward. (14-16)

Since this world is governed by the law of trials, the righteous and the wicked are mixed, and often the wrongdoers hold dominance, which is why those without spiritual vision fail to see its purposeful nature. But on the Day of Judgment, the division will occur, separating the believers from the disbelievers. Those who believed and did righteous deeds will be in a grand garden, delighted, while those who disbelieved and rejected His signs and the meeting of the hereafter will be brought forward in punishment. The indefinite form of 'garden' (rawdati) denotes its grandeur. 'Yuhbaroon' means to be delighted, indicating that Allah will provide all resources for their joy. Regarding the criminals, 'brought forward' (muhdaroon) is used to depict their complete helplessness, meaning they will be brought forward in custody like convicted prisoners.

So exalted is Allah when you reach the evening and when you reach the morning. And to Him belongs praise throughout the heavens and the earth and at night and when you are at noon. (17-18)

This defines the response required of humans to His absolute authority and His law of recompense: everyone should glorify Him in the evening, the morning, at night, and at noon.

'And to Him belongs praise throughout the heavens and the earth' (Wa lahul-hamdu fis-samawati wal-ard) is a parenthetical reminder, implying: 'This call to glorify Him is not a foreign call; rather, the entire universe is glorifying His praise. Those who worship others are out of harmony with the universe, whereas those who glorify Him align themselves with the whole creation.' It also expresses selflessness, showing that He is not in need of their glorification, as the entire universe is already filled with His praise.

Reflecting on these specified times shows that Allah has chosen those moments for His glorification when His grand signs are manifested—e.g., when night enters day, day enters night, the sun declines from its zenith, or the night turns dark. Anyone who reflects is naturally moved by these changes to remember the One by whose command these shifts occur; if someone remains unmindful at these times, he is spiritually dead.

The times of prayer—specifically Fajr and Zuhr—are explicitly mentioned here. If 'evening' (tumsoon) is taken to include Asr and Maghrib, and 'night' (ashiyyan) to include Isha, then all five daily prayers are encompassed here. Since 'ashiyy' is used in Arabic for both the sun's decline and the period from sunset to nightfall, using it for Isha is linguistically correct.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

He brings the living out of the dead and brings the dead out of the living and brings to life the earth after its death. And thus you will be brought out. (19)

'Ikhraj' means to bring out or make manifest. This refutes the deniers' doubt regarding the Resurrection: you treat it as highly improbable, yet you witness the living arising from the dead, and death overtaking the living. As an example: you see the earth dead and dry, but Allah revives it with a single rain shower. In the same manner, you will be resurrected after death. Life and death are in His power; neither is causing death difficult for Him, nor is restoring life. The rehearsal of the Resurrection is constantly occurring before your eyes. Although the focus is on حیات بعد الموت (life after death), the transition from life to death is also mentioned to show His absolute power. This has been explained in Surah al-An'am, verses 95-96.

And of His signs is that He created you from dust; then, suddenly you were human beings dispersing. (20)

This means that the realities you are called to believe in are fully based on His power and wisdom; why then do you demand external signs? The greatest sign is within your own creation: He created you from lifeless dust, and suddenly you became living, intelligent beings spread across the earth. 'Suddenly' (idha) draws attention to this grand display of power: from lifeless dust, His power populated a whole world of living beings! If such an intelligent human being still disputes how God can resurrect him, it is the height of foolishness. This is expressed elsewhere as: 'We created human beings, and suddenly he became an open adversary, challenging Our power!'

And of His signs is that He created for you from yourselves mates that you may find tranquility in them; and He placed between you affection and mercy. Indeed in that are signs for a people who reflect. (21)

Advancing a step further, the text points to another grand sign: He created for you mates from your own kind to find tranquility in them, and placed affection and mercy between you. It states that there are diverse signs in this for those who reflect.

First: It shows that Allah has created everything in pairs, and every object finds its fulfillment through its pair. This suggests that this world also has a pair—the hereafter—which completes its purpose.

Second: It shows that our Creator is All-Merciful and Loving: He placed the desire for a partner in our nature, created a partner from our own kind, and placed affection and mercy between us to unite us.

Third: It shows that the diverse, opposite elements in this universe display deep harmony for a higher purpose, proving that there is only one Creator who maintains this harmony.

Fourth: It refutes the foolish idea that the universe evolved on its own; if it did, where did this amazing harmony among opposite elements come from? This is clear proof that an All-Wise, All-Powerful Lord is running this entire system.

And of His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and the diversity of your languages and your colors. Indeed in that are signs for those of knowledge. (22)

This points to the same reality from another angle: if people reflect, they will see unity within diversity. The heavens and the earth are vastly distant, yet they display such deep harmony that none can assume they were created by separate gods or are governed by separate wills. Their mutual harmony proves that one Creator is running both for a common purpose. Similarly, among humans, languages and colors are diverse, yet none can suggest that different colors or languages belong to separate creators; rather, one Creator has created everyone. It is His amazing craftsmanship that He made every human's voice, features, and color unique, so that no two individuals among millions are identical.

'Indeed in that are signs for those of knowledge' (Inna fee dhalika la-aayatin lil-'alimeen). The previous verse referenced 'those who reflect' (yatafakkaroon), and here it references 'those of knowledge' ('alimeen). This shows that true scholars are those who reflect on His signs and see unity within diversity and harmony within opposites, concluding that this grand universe must have a purposeful destination—the Resurrection—to reward the righteous and punish the wicked.

This verse also makes it clear that differences in language and color are not meant to divide humans. These physical differences are not the basis of division. Just as all humans are God's creation despite these differences, they should live under one divine system. Those who treat race, color, or language as the basis of division are attacking the unity of the universe, which is a form of polytheism and corruption on earth.

And of His signs is your sleep by night and day and your seeking of His bounty. Indeed in that are signs for a people who listen. (23)

'Your seeking of His bounty' is conjoined to 'your sleep,' meaning: of His signs is that He made for you the night to sleep and the day to seek His provision. This has passed in previous Surahs.

'Indeed in that are signs for a people who listen' (Inna fee dhalika la-aayatin li-qawmin yasma'oon) means there are signs in this for those who listen attentively. The arguments for monotheism in the harmony of night and day, and the indication toward the Resurrection in waking up, have been explained, but these signs benefit only those who listen to His call. This is a rebuke to those who are busy opposing rather than listening.

The previous verses referenced 'reflect' and 'knowledge,' and here they reference 'listening.' While its status is below the first two, it is still a source of guidance: if a person has enough decency to listen to wise counsel, he can find guidance even if he lacks the capacity for deep reflection.

And of His signs is that He shows you the lightning [causing] fear and hope, and He sends down from the sky water by which He gives life to the earth after its death. Indeed in that are signs for a people who use reason. (24)

And of His signs is the lightning, carrying both hope and fear: it serves as glad tidings of rain, and also as a warning of His wrath. The first is common, and the second is recorded in history—e.g., the shriek (sa'iqah) that destroyed 'Ad and Thamud. There are signs in this for those who use reason:

First: Both mercy and punishment are in His hand; therefore, a servant must turn to Him in all states of hope and fear. The polytheists' belief in separate deities of mercy and punishment is false.

Second: Allah is not indifferent to His creation; He runs all affairs and deals out reward or punishment in accordance with His justice.

Third: If He wills to punish a nation, He does not require separate weapons; He can easily turn His blessings into a source of punishment.

Regarding the revival of the dead land, its arguments have been explained under verse 19.

And of His signs is that the heaven and the earth remain by His command. Then when He calls you with a single call from the earth, at once you will come out. And to Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth. All to Him are obedient. (25-26)

Let none assume that the massive system of the heavens and the earth cannot be shaken for the Resurrection. The Quraysh used to ask how these firm mountains could be moved, treating the Resurrection as an imaginary threat. The text states that the heavens and the earth stand by His command, and will remain only as long as He wills. When He wills to fold this system and establish another, He will simply summon you with a single call, and you will immediately come out of the earth. He does not need to call repeatedly; a single call is sufficient. This is expressed elsewhere as: 'And the earth hearkens to her Lord, and it is fit for her to do so.'

'And to Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth. All to Him are obedient' (Wa lahu man fis-samawati wal-ard; kullul-lahu qanitoon) is the proof: since everything in the heavens and the earth is His creation and is obedient to Him, none can refuse His command or call.

And He is the One who originates creation; then He repeats it, and that is easier for Him. And to Him belongs the highest attribute in the heavens and the earth. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise. (27)

This is the proof for 'at once you will come out' (verse 25), and also introduces the upcoming discourse on monotheism. The argument for repeating creation appeared in verse 11.

Here, the prefix 'and that is easier for Him' (wa Huwa ahwanu 'alayh) is added: since He originated creation without any difficulty, repeating it is even simpler. This refutes the deniers' doubt who treated the Resurrection as highly improbable: when origination is not difficult, why should repetition be so? Repetition is simpler than origination.

'And to Him belongs the highest attribute in the heavens and the earth' (wa lahul-mathalul-a'la fis-samawati wal-ard). 'Mathal' here means attribute. This word is used for Allah's attributes because they are described in human language to make them close to our understanding, though they carry the highest meaning worthy of His glory.

It states that He alone deserves the highest attributes in the heavens and the earth; none can match Him. This is coupled with the attributes 'Al-Aziz' (Mighty) and 'Al-Hakeem' (Wise)—showing that He is dominant over all things and wise in His plans. This leads to the logical conclusion: since none matches Him in His attributes, none can match Him in His rights.

He presents to you an example from yourselves. Do you have among those whom your right hands possess any partners in what We have provided you so that you are equal therein [and] you fear them as you fear each other? Thus We detail the verses for a people who use reason. (28)

To refute polytheism, the text points to an argument from their own conduct. It states: 'We present an example from your own lives. Do you accept your own slaves as equal partners in the wealth We have provided you, so that you consult and fear them as you consult your equal partners?' 'Fear' (khawf) here means to respect and consult, as explained elsewhere.

Meaning, since you do not accept your own slaves as equal partners in the wealth We have granted you, how can you associate His creatures with Him in His divinity! It is highly absurd that you associate with Him what you do not accept for yourselves. This is similar to the psychological argument that they associated daughters with Allah though they hated daughters for themselves. The core argument is: leaving aside all cosmic arguments, their own conduct refutes their polytheism, for they associate with Him what they hold in contempt for themselves.

'Thus We detail the verses for a people who use reason' (kadhalika nufassilul-aayati li-qawmin ya'qiloon) means the argument is fully clear, but it benefits only those who use their reason, not those who have closed their minds.

Rather, those who wrong follow their desires without knowledge. Then who can guide him whom Allah has sent astray? And for them there are no helpers. (29)

'Those who wrong' (alladheena zalamoo) refers to the polytheists who commit the grave injustice of polytheism. It states that these people do not use their reason, but simply follow their own desires and innovations. 'Without knowledge' (bighayri 'ilm) means they have no proof or argument, but follow their desires blindly.

'Then who can guide him whom Allah has sent astray?' means those who ignore their intellect and follow their desires are left by Allah to wander blindly. 'And for them there are no helpers' (wa ma lahum-min-nasireen) means they will spend their lives in this deviation, and none can save them from their ultimate end in this world or the hereafter.

4. The upcoming subject... Verses 30-39

The previous verses refuted the objections of the polytheists who used the historical events to mock the call. Now, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his Companions are commanded to remain firm on the true, natural religion (Islam) and establish its two pillars—prayer and charity—while the Quraysh are warned that their moral and physical corruption has brought them to the brink of ruin. They will soon face their end, while those who stand firm on the straight path will be rewarded out of His grace. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

So direct your face toward the religion, inclining to truth. [Adhere to] the fitrah of Allah upon which He has created people. No change should there be in the creation of Allah. That is the correct religion, but most of the people do not know. (30)

Although the verb is singular, addressing the Prophet as the representative of the community (as shown by the plural verbs in the subsequent verse), the command is: 'Direct your face with complete focus toward the true religion, ignoring their objections.' 'The religion' (ad-Deen) refers to Islam—the true religion of all prophets, completed through the unlettered Prophet. This is the only true religion: 'Indeed, the religion in the sight of Allah is Islam.' 'Inclining to truth' (haneefan) means turning away from all false ways, just as Abraham (peace be upon him) did. This word is specifically used for Abraham, reminding them of the complete focus and commitment displayed by him.

'The fitrah of Allah upon which He has created people' (fitratallahil-latee fataran-nasa 'alayha). A verb is omitted here, meaning: 'Adhere to this natural religion upon which the Creator has created humans.' This style serves to focus all attention on the object. This is the proof of the truth of this religion: it is not a foreign burden imposed upon you, but is the very expression of your own nature. This refutes the idea of those philosophers who claim that humans are born as a blank slate and are entirely the product of their environment. Allah has created humans on a sound nature, endowing them with the recognition of good and evil and the desire for virtue, though they possess the choice to follow their desires and distort their own nature.

The prophets were sent not because humans lacked the recognition of good and evil, but because they could face various illusions and difficulties in understanding all the requirements of their natural guidance. The teachings of the prophets are based on the very principles embedded in human nature, which is why the sincere recognized them immediately, and only those who had distorted their nature opposed them. We have discussed this in detail in Surah Hud and Surah an-Noor. The Qur'an being described as a 'reminder' (dhikr / dhikra) also carries this meaning—reminding humans of what lies within their own nature.

'No change should there be in the creation of Allah' (la tabdeela li-khalqillah). The negation denotes prohibition rather than impossibility. 'Creation' here refers to His natural religion.

It means that it is not permissible to alter the natural order established by the Creator. He who created everything knows best its purpose and requirements; none has the right to alter it. Doing so only leads to corruption, much like placing eyes on the heel instead of the forehead, or trying to reverse the natural roles of men and women. The same applies to the natural religion: human success depends on adhering to its principles. Deviating from it leads to ultimate ruin, regardless of how advanced the deviators claim to be.

'That is the correct religion' (dhalikad-deenul-qayyim) means this is the straight path, directly connected to human nature and intellect, without any deviation.

'But most of the people do not know' (wa lakinna aktharannasi la ya'lamoon) means the majority do not understand this reality, which is why they have corrupted the natural religion and persist in their deviation.

[Adhere to it], turning in repentance to Him, and fear Him and establish prayer and do not be of those who associate partners with Him. (31)

'Turning in repentance to Him' (muneebeena ilayh) is a state referring to the pronoun in 'direct your face.' As noted, although the direct verb was singular, this plural state clarifies that the command is to the entire community. It means that intellectual focus alone is not sufficient; rather, it must be accompanied by humility, repentance, and a complete adherence to His limits.

'And establish prayer' (wa aqeemus-salat). Prayer is a fundamental pillar of the correct religion, serving as the protector of this commitment and humility.

'And do not be of those who associate partners with Him' is a warning to stay away from the way of the polytheists, who have destroyed these fundamental pillars.

[Or] of those who have divided their religion and become sects, every faction rejoicing in what it has. (32)

This further describes the polytheists whose way the believers are warned to stay away from. This applies directly to the Quraysh, who had divided the monotheistic religion of Abraham and Ishmael into various sects, associating partners with Him. They gathered hundreds of idols in the Kaaba, and every faction was so obsessed with its own idol that they refused to listen to any truth. This warning is addressed to the believers to express complete aversion toward the polytheists, as if they are no longer worthy of being addressed directly.

And when adversity touches the people, they call upon their Lord, turning in repentance to Him; then when He lets them taste mercy from Him, at once a party of them associate partners with their Lord, so they may be ungrateful for what We have given them. So enjoy yourselves, for you are going to know. (33-34)

This is a direct warning to the polytheists: when they face adversity, they call upon Him with complete humility, but the moment He blesses them with His mercy, they return to their polytheism. This has passed in previous Surahs and will appear in Surah Luqman, verse 32.

'So they may be ungrateful for what We have given them. So enjoy yourselves, for you are going to know' is identical to Surah al-Ankabut, verse 66. They receive blessings from Him, yet direct their gratitude to others. After this, they are addressed in a stern tone: enjoy yourselves for a time, but you will soon face the consequence of your ingratitude.

Or have We sent down to them an authority (sultan) that speaks of what they associate with Him? (35)

We have explained this rhetorical question where some words are omitted, meaning: 'Have they associated partners with Him on their own, or have We revealed a clear proof that speaks of the validity of their partners?' This style carries intense sarcasm and wrath.

These verses point to a powerful psychological argument: in times of absolute helplessness, human nature instinctively turns to the one true God, leaving aside all false deities, proving that human nature carries no testimony for polytheism. However, once the adversity passes, unmindfulness returns, and they slip back into deviation.

And when We let the people taste mercy, they rejoice in it, and if evil afflicts them for what their hands have put forth, at once they despair. (36)

Meaning, when He blesses them with His mercy, they should show gratitude, and when He subjects them to trials because of their own misdeeds, they should show patience, but very few show gratitude and patience. The majority turn arrogant when blessed, and become completely despondent when afflicted. While these are general descriptions of human behavior, they were directed at the Quraysh, warning them that if they persist in their pride, they will face His grasp, and will then cry in vain.

'For what their hands have put forth' (bi-ma qaddamat aydeehim) shows that the afflictions people face in this world are often a result of their own misdeeds. Regarding the wicked, His law is as stated here, but the righteous and the prophets also face trials as a means of spiritual training and elevation.

Do they not see that Allah extends provision for whom He wills and restricts it? Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe. (37)

Meaning, have they not reflected on the fact that the extension and restriction of provision are entirely in His hands, based on His wisdom? Therefore, none has the right to turn arrogant when blessed, or despair when restricted. He tests His servants through both states, and the final success will be determined in the hereafter.

'Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe' (Inna fee dhalika la-aayatin li-qawmin yu'minoon) means there are signs in this for those who believe:

First: All resources are in His hands alone; He alone extends or restricts provision.

Second: Worldly prosperity is no proof of success, nor is poverty a proof of failure; both are states of trial, and the final decision rests in the hereafter.

Third: These states serve to test the servant's gratitude and patience—the source of all human virtues.

So give the relative his right, and the needy, and the traveler. That is best for those who desire the countenance of Allah, and those are the successful. (38)

In verse 31, the first pillar of the natural religion—prayer—was mentioned. After some intermediate warnings, the text returns to the second pillar—charity. It was warned that those blessed with prosperity have no right to treat it as their own achievement, but should show gratitude to Him.

This verse defines the way of showing gratitude: give the relatives, the needy, and the travelers their right. 'His right' (haqqahu) indicates that a person's prosperity contains the rights of others, which are placed in his custody as a trust; therefore, he must fulfill them. If he fails, he is guilty of violating their rights. This is the only way for those who seek the pleasure of Allah, and they are the successful. It also implies that those who hoard wealth for pride and luxury will find it a source of ruin.

And what you give in usury (riba) to increase within the wealth of people will not increase with Allah. But what you give in charity (zakat), desiring the countenance of Allah - those are the multipliers. (39)

'Riba' refers to lending money on interest to increase wealth. Those who have translated it as gifts or presents are incorrect. This is an example of naming a thing by its ultimate consequence.

'Zakat' here is used in the general sense of charity, not the specific tax, as is used in other places in the Qur'an.

This describes the contrasting conduct: those who, instead of spending for His pleasure, lend money on interest so that their wealth increases by consuming the wealth of others. They should know that such wealth may appear to increase in this world, but holds no value or growth in the sight of Allah; rather, He obliterates it. Growth is only for the wealth spent for His pleasure.

This comparison shows that usury is like a parasite that grows by consuming others, carrying no true growth or blessing. True blessing is only for the wealth spent for His pleasure. People hoard wealth out of concern for the future, but their vision is limited to this life; the true future is the hereafter, and wealth is beneficial only when spent in His cause.

It should also be noted that usury is fundamentally different from trade: trade involves risk and effort, whereas usury is a parasitic practice of taking interest without taking any risk, which is why Islam has blessed trade and prohibited usury.

6. The upcoming subject... Verses 40-60

Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, warning the opponents that they have destroyed the pillars of the natural religion—monotheism and compassion—and have thus filled the earth with corruption. This corruption makes them deserve His grasp, so that they may taste the consequence of their actions. Subsequently, the believers are promised victory, and the signs of the universe are used to show that He can easily turn difficult situations into ease. Along with this, the Prophet and his Companions are commanded to stand firm on the natural religion and ignore those who have turned blind and deaf. Read the upcoming verses in this light.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Allah is the One who created you, then provided for you, then He will cause you to die, then He will give you life. Is there any of your "partners" who does any of that? Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him. (40)

The Quraysh tried to mock the promises of victory by pointing to the Persian victory, which has been refuted. Now, they are addressed directly: do not indulge in these illusions, but face the realities. The corruption you have spread has brought you to the brink of ruin, and your partners cannot save you. It asks: 'Did your partners create you, or provide for you, or cause you to die, or will they resurrect you? When they have no share in any of these, what is the basis for relying on them?'

'Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him' (Subhanahu wa ta'ala 'amma yushrikoon) is addressed turning away from them, expressing absolute disapproval.

Corruption has appeared throughout the land and sea by [reason of] what the hands of people have earned so He may let them taste some of [the consequence of] what they have done that perhaps they will return. (41)

Just as 'night and day' denote encompassment, 'land and sea' (al-barr wal-bahr) denote the complete encompassment of corruption in every aspect of life. Sincerity of intellect depends on faith, and uprightness of action depends on Islam; these two are linked, and when they are destroyed, corruption dominates every aspect of life. Any temporary prosperity in such a state is a mere illusion.

'So He may let them taste some of what they have done' (li-yudheeqahum ba'dal-ladhee 'amiloo) describes the consequence: when people fill every aspect of life with corruption, they invite His warning. Consequently, He lets them taste some of the consequences of their deeds to warn them to return to the straight path.

It should be noted that there are two types of punishments: warnings and final destruction. Here, the Quraysh are warned of the first, implying: 'Your corruption has made you deserve a warning; if you do not reform, the final destruction will inevitably strike you.'

Say, "Travel through the land and observe how was the end of those before. Most of them were polytheists." (42)

Meaning: 'Tell them not to indulge in false hopes, but observe the ruins of past nations. They were also polytheists, and when they rejected the warnings, Allah destroyed them, saving only the small group of believers.'

So direct your face toward the correct religion before there comes a Day from Allah which cannot be averted. That Day they will be partitioned. (43)

This is a transition addressed to the Prophet and his Companions, similar to verse 31: 'Do not pay attention to their objections; remain firm on the correct religion and prepare for the Day which cannot be averted.' 'That Day they will be partitioned' (Yawma'idhing-yassadda'oon) means while they rely on their unity and partners today, on that Day they will be separated, and none can help another.

Whoever disbelieves - upon him is his disbelief, and whoever does righteousness - they are preparing for themselves. (44)

On that Day, everyone will receive recompense in accordance with his deeds: the burden of disbelief will be borne by the disbelievers alone, and those who do righteous deeds will prepare their own eternal comfort.

That He may reward those who have believed and done righteous deeds out of His bounty. Indeed, He does not love the disbelievers. (45)

This is conjoined to 'before there comes a Day' (verse 43): the purpose of that Day is to reward the believers out of His bounty. This shows that the primary purpose of the Resurrection is to reward the believers, while the punishment of the disbelievers is its logical consequence. 'Out of His bounty' (min fadlih) indicates that the reward of the believers will not be measured by their deeds alone, but will be according to His boundless grace.

'Indeed, He does not love the disbelievers' (Innahu la yuhibbul-kafireen) carries an intense tone of disapproval. For the believers, the entire Resurrection is established to honor them, while the disbelievers are dismissed as being unloved by Him, destined for Hell.

And of His signs is that He sends the winds as glad tidings and to let you taste of His mercy and that the ships may sail by His command and that you may seek of His bounty, and perhaps you will be grateful. (46)

This and the subsequent verses console the Prophet and the Muslims: just as He sends the winds as glad tidings of His rain of mercy when the air is still and hot, He will turn these challenging situations into ease and success for you. 'And that the ships may sail by His command' means He has made these winds a means for your travel and trade, 'and that you may seek of His bounty, and perhaps you will be grateful'—showing that He has established this system for your benefit so that you may remain grateful to Him. This also warnings the Muslims: when you receive His victory and support, do not turn unmindful, but remain grateful to Him.

And We already sent before you messengers to their people, and they came to them with clear evidences; then We took retribution from those who committed crimes, and support of the believers was a duty upon Us. (47)

This historical fact supports the promise: 'You are not the first messenger; We sent messengers before you who came with clear proofs, but their nations chose the path of crime and opposed them. Consequently, We took retribution from the criminals, for supporting the believers is a duty upon Us.' This is the promise of your victory; rest assured.

Allah is the One who sends the winds, so they stir the clouds and He spreads them in the sky however He wills, and He makes them fragments so you see the rain emerge from within them. And when He causes it to fall upon whom He wills of His servants, at once they rejoice, although they were, before it was sent down upon them, despondent. (48-49)

Since the promise of victory was made at a time when the apparent situation was completely adverse, Allah pointed to a daily phenomenon of His power and mercy to reassure the believers: 'He sends the winds to stir the clouds, spreads them in the sky, and makes them fragments so that rain emerges from within them. When He causes it to fall upon His servants, they immediately rejoice, though they were completely despondent just moments before.' Meaning, His mercy will manifest for you in the same manner; though success seems far off now, when His mercy descends, you will immediately rejoice.

There is no repetition in 'before it' (min qabli an) and 'before him' (min qablih); they refer to two distinct states: they were despondent before the rain, and before the signs of rain appeared.

So observe the effects of the mercy of Allah - how He gives life to the earth after its death. Indeed, that [same One] will give life to the dead, and He is over all things competent. (50)

The mention of the Resurrection here is to console the believers: their ultimate hope lies in the hereafter, so after pointing to their worldly success, the text points to the final manifestation of His mercy. Just as He revives the dead land, He will resurrect the dead. He is competent over all things. The deniers mock your faith in the Resurrection, but His mercy will surely manifest, and they will then face their ruin. The Resurrection is indeed a requirement of His attribute of mercy, established to reward the believers. This has passed in Surah al-An'am, verse 12.

And if We sent a wind and they saw it [their crop] turned yellow, they would remain thereafter disbelieving. (51)

This is a comment on verses 48-49: 'Such is the inconsistent nature of these people: they lose hope at the slightest delay, rejoice when blessed, and if We send a wind that turns their crops yellow, they immediately return to their ingratitude and disbelief.' You cannot expect any consistency from such people; your message will benefit only those who are grateful in ease and patient in trial.

We have explained the difference between 'riyah' (favorable winds) and 'reeh' (destructive wind). The pronoun in 'they saw it' refers to their crops, as is clear from the context.

So indeed, you cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf hear the call when they turn their backs, fleeing. And you cannot guide the blind away from their error. You will only make hear those who believe in Our verses, so they are Muslims in submission. (52-53)

Those who are so inconsistent that they swing from pride to despair, and whose hearts remain unaffected by His signs, are spiritually dead, deaf, and blind. You cannot guide them, nor is it your duty to do so. 'Nor can you make the deaf hear the call when they turn their backs, fleeing' means even the deaf might understand some signs if they pay attention, but these people are both deaf and fleeing, so they cannot be guided. 'And you cannot guide the blind away from their error' (Wa ma anta bi-hadiyal-'umyi 'an dalalatihim) means you cannot turn them away from their error to the straight path.

'You will only make hear those who believe in Our verses' (In tusmi'u illa man yu'minu bi-aayatina) means your message will benefit only those who accept the signs of the universe and submit to their Lord.

Allah is the One who created you from weakness, then made after weakness strength, then made after strength weakness and white hair. He creates what He wills, and He is the Knowing, the Competent. (54)

This is a final warning: none should feel proud of his strength or resources, as everything is granted by Him and will return to Him. Reflect on your own creation: He created you in a state of absolute weakness, blessed you with the strength of youth, and then returned you to weakness and old age. None can preserve his youth or strength; everything is in His power, and He is the All-Knowing, the Competent.

And the Day the Hour is established the criminals will swear they had not remained but an hour. Thus they were deluded. (55)

This is another warning: do not treat this worldly life as permanent. On the Day of Judgment, when the criminals are resurrected, they will swear they remained on earth for only an hour. Today, they are unmindful, thinking the Resurrection is far off, but on that Day, they will feel that all these stages passed in the blink of an eye.

'Thus they were deluded' (kadhalika kanoo yu'fakoon) means they never understood the actual value of their life; they treated the Resurrection as far off and neglected it completely.

But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, "You remained in the decree of Allah until the Day of Resurrection, and this is the Day of Resurrection, but you did not know." (56)

'Those who were given knowledge and faith' refers to those who used their reason in this world and believed in His signs. They will correct the criminals: 'Your estimate is wrong; in accordance with the decree of Allah, you remained until the Day of Resurrection, and this is that Day, but you chose to ignore it.' This shows that those who live their lives with focus on the hereafter will remain composed on the Day of Judgment, realizing they have reached their destination.

So that Day their excuse will not benefit those who wronged, nor will they be asked to appease [Allah]. (57)

Meaning, on that Day, no excuse will benefit them, nor will they be granted another opportunity to please their Lord. The opportunity for action was granted in this world, and those who wasted it will find no second chance; prepare for it today.

And We have certainly presented to the people in this Qur'an of every kind of example. And if you should bring them a sign, those who disbelieve will surely say, "You are not but falsifiers." (58)

'Presenting an example' refers to explaining all wisdom and guidance through parables. The Prophet is told: 'We have explained every kind of wisdom in this Qur'an, so present it to them. If they do not accept it, even if you show them a major physical miracle, they will dismiss it as mere falsehood and magic.'

Thus does Allah seal the hearts of those who do not know. (59)

Meaning, those who choose to close their ears and hearts to His guidance are sealed by His law, so no sign can benefit them.

So be patient. Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth. And let them not disquiet you who are not certain. (60)

'Estikhfaf' means to treat someone as light, dismissing his words as weightless. In highly reassuring terms, the Prophet is commanded: 'Remain firm on your stance. The promise of victory Allah has made is absolutely true; let not their mockery and denial weaken your stance or make you feel hesitant. If you stand firm, they will be the ones who are humiliated and proved weightless, and all their efforts to shake your resolve will fail.'

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed by the grace of Allah. Praise be to Allah for this.
